1.synthesis: Isaiah 27 opens with a declaration that the chaotic powers of the cosmos will be finally defeated: “In that day the LORD with his hard and great and strong sword will punish Leviathan the fleeing serpent, Leviathan the twisting serpent, and he will slay the dragon that is in the sea” (27:1). The triple description of Leviathan—fleeing serpent, twisting serpent, dragon in the sea—draws on the ancient Near Eastern mythology of the chaos monster (Ugaritic Lotan, Babylonian Tiamat), the primordial force of disorder that opposed divine creation. YHWH’s hard, great, strong sword is the instrument of final victory: the weapon against the monster is precise and decisive. The single verse functions as the eschatological preface to the chapter: all that follows (the vineyard song, the gathering of Israel) takes place in the context of the final defeat of the chaos-power that threatened YHWH’s creation and his people.
1.historical_context: The Leviathan tradition in the Old Testament appears at several key points: Job 41 (the divine speech about Leviathan as the archetype of what humans cannot control), Psalm 74:14 (YHWH crushing Leviathan’s heads), and Psalm 104:26 (Leviathan playing in the sea). The Ugaritic texts describe the storm-god Baal’s victory over Lotan “the fleeing serpent” and “the twisting serpent”—the exact language Isaiah 27:1 uses for Leviathan. Isaiah appropriates this mythological tradition to describe YHWH’s eschatological victory: the ancient combat myth becomes the vehicle for announcing the final defeat of chaos in its ultimate form.
1.literary_structure: The single verse functions as a literary overture to the chapter: it announces the cosmic dimension of the eschatological act (Leviathan slain) before the pastoral and historical dimensions (the vineyard restored, Israel gathered) are addressed. The escalating triple description of Leviathan (fleeing / twisting / dragon) suggests completeness: every form of the chaos-serpent is included in the divine judgment.
1.theological_application: The slaying of Leviathan is the eschatological defeat of everything that has opposed God’s creative and redemptive purposes: the chaos-power behind the nations’ opposition to YHWH, the spiritual force of disorder that has threatened the people of God throughout history. Communities shaped by this verse live in the confidence that the chaos-serpent is not the equal and opposite of YHWH but is subject to his decisive judgment: the hard, great, strong sword is already raised, and the day of its use is coming.
1.christology: The dragon in the sea slain by YHWH’s sword (27:1) is taken up in Revelation 12:9 and 20:2 as Satan, “that ancient serpent,” the dragon who is bound and finally cast into the lake of fire. Christ is the one who has the authority to slay the serpent: at the cross, “through death he might destroy the one who has the power of death, that is, the devil” (Heb 2:14). The sword of the LORD that slays Leviathan is the word of Christ: “the sword of the Spirit, which is the word of God” (Eph 6:17), and the sharp two-edged sword from the mouth of the Son of Man in Revelation 1:16.

2.synthesis: In direct contrast to the vineyard song of Isaiah 5 (which announced judgment on a vineyard that failed to produce), Isaiah 27:2–11 sings a new vineyard song: “In that day, ‘A pleasant vineyard, sing of it! I, the LORD, am its keeper; every moment I water it. Lest anyone punish it, I keep it night and day; I have no wrath’” (27:2–4). The contrast is total: the song of chapter 5 ended with the vineyard broken down, its wall torn away, given over to briers and thorns; the song of chapter 27 has YHWH himself as keeper, watering it every moment, watching over it night and day without wrath. If there are briers and thorns, he will march against them and burn them together (27:4). Or let the enemy take hold of YHWH’s protection, make peace with him, and make peace with YHWH (27:5). Jacob will take root; Israel will blossom and put forth shoots and fill the whole world with fruit (27:6). The measure of judgment on Israel is compared favorably to the measure on their oppressors (v. 7); YHWH has expelled Israel by exile, but the oracle is for atonement: “This is all the fruit: the removal of his sin” (27:9).
2.historical_context: The deliberate contrast with Isaiah 5’s vineyard song is the key interpretive frame: chapter 5 announced the vineyard’s destruction; chapter 27 announces its restoration. The “I have no wrath” declaration of v. 4 is the eschatological overcoming of the wrath that drove the judgment of chapter 5. The fruit that fills the whole world (v. 6) is Israel’s eschatological role as the source of blessing to the nations—the fulfillment of the Abrahamic promise. The “removal of his sin” as the fruit of the exile (v. 9) interprets the judgment as having a purgative purpose: the exile purges the idolatry that the vineyard had harbored.
2.literary_structure: The vineyard song (vv. 2–5) is followed by the agricultural vision of Jacob’s global fruitfulness (v. 6) followed by the measured comparison of Israel’s discipline with that of its oppressors (vv. 7–8) followed by the theological purpose of the exile (v. 9). The structure is: YHWH as tender keeper → Israel’s fruitfulness → Israel’s lesser discipline → the exile’s atonement purpose. The vineyard is now tended rather than abandoned; the fruit is now global rather than absent.
2.theological_application: The reversal of the vineyard song—from YHWH withdrawing his protection (Isaiah 5) to YHWH becoming the keeper who waters every moment without wrath (Isaiah 27)—is the eschatological fulfillment of covenant renewal. Communities shaped by this passage understand the church as the vineyard tended by the Lord who has no wrath against those for whom atonement has been made (v. 9). The global fruitfulness of v. 6 is the missionary vision: the restored vineyard does not produce only for itself but fills the whole world with fruit.
2.christology: The vineyard that Jacob/Israel will become, filling the whole world with fruit (27:6), is fulfilled in Christ, who declares himself the true vine: “I am the true vine, and my Father is the vinedresser” (John 15:1). The branches are those who abide in him and bear fruit. The “removal of his sin” as the fruit of the exile (v. 9) is the type of the definitive removal of sin accomplished at the cross: Christ “appeared once for all at the end of the ages to put away sin by the sacrifice of himself” (Heb 9:26). The vineyard tended without wrath is the church tended by Christ who has borne the wrath in himself.

12.synthesis: Isaiah 27 closes with a vision of the final gathering of Israel from the far reaches of the diaspora: “In that day from the river Euphrates to the Brook of Egypt the LORD will thresh out the grain, and you will be gleaned one by one, O people of Israel” (27:12). The geographical scope is the full extent of the ancient Near Eastern world: from the Euphrates (northeast) to the Brook of Egypt (southwest)—the borders that define the land promised to Abraham (Gen 15:18). The threshing imagery continues the agricultural metaphor of the vineyard: YHWH threshes the grain to separate the precious from the worthless and gathers the Israelites one by one, each individually known and collected. The closing verse: “And in that day a great trumpet will be blown, and those who were lost in the land of Assyria and those who were driven out to the land of Egypt will come and worship the LORD on the holy mountain at Jerusalem” (27:13). The great trumpet sounds, the lost are found, the driven-out return, and the gathering ends at Jerusalem in worship on the holy mountain.
12.historical_context: The two great directions of Israel’s exile are Assyria (north, the direction of the 722 BC fall of Samaria) and Egypt (south, where many fled after the 586 BC fall of Jerusalem). The gathering from these two endpoints encompasses all of Israel’s diaspora. The great trumpet (shofar gadol) is the eschatological summons: as the Jubilee was announced by the trumpet (Lev 25:9) and Sinai’s theophany was accompanied by trumpets (Exod 19:19), so the final gathering is announced by the great eschatological trumpet. The worship on the holy mountain at Jerusalem is the fulfillment of the Zion theology that runs throughout Isaiah.
12.literary_structure: The two-verse closing is a distillation of the entire Isaiah Apocalypse’s vision: after the cosmic judgment (ch. 24), the remnant’s praise (ch. 24), the feast for all peoples (ch. 25), the resurrection (ch. 26), and the slaying of Leviathan and restoration of the vineyard (ch. 27:1–11), the end point is: the great trumpet sounds and the lost return to worship on the holy mountain. Everything in the Apocalypse moves toward this: worship in Jerusalem by the gathered people of God.
12.theological_application: The gathering one by one (27:12) is the pastoral particularity of the eschatological harvest: the divine threshing is not indiscriminate but collects each individual, known and precious. Communities shaped by this verse understand that the eschatological hope is not merely corporate but irreducibly personal: each person is gleaned, one by one. The great trumpet that summons the lost (27:13) is the announcement that no exile is permanent, no displacement is final: those who were lost and driven out are found and gathered to worship.
12.christology: The great trumpet of 27:13 is taken up by Jesus in his eschatological discourse: “And he will send out his angels with a loud trumpet call, and they will gather his elect from the four winds, from one end of heaven to the other” (Matt 24:31). Paul applies it to the resurrection of the dead: “For the Lord himself will descend from heaven with a cry of command, with the voice of an archangel, and with the sound of the trumpet of God” (1 Thess 4:16). The lost who were in Assyria and Egypt and who come to worship on the holy mountain are the elect gathered by Christ’s angels at his coming—and the holy mountain where they worship is the heavenly Jerusalem, Mount Zion, the city of the living God (Heb 12:22).
